summary: Deprecated audit trail for DefaultDynamicHeteroCpuPolicy. The current app writes HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Session Manager\Kernel\DefaultDynamicHeteroCpuPolicy = 3, but this research pass did not capture a primary Microsoft source that maps the power-policy semantics onto the kernel registry key the app writes.

setting.name: Default Dynamic Heterogeneous CPU Policy
setting.casual_explanation: This appears to be a low-level policy for how Windows prefers performant versus efficient CPU cores on hybrid systems. It is not a normal consumer-facing tweak.
setting.why_people_change_it: People would only touch it when experimenting with hybrid-core scheduling behavior or undoing manual kernel registry changes from advanced tweak guides.
setting.tradeoffs_overview: Changing heterogeneous scheduling policy can affect where threads run on systems with different classes of CPU cores. Microsoft publishes official value meanings for related scheduling policies, but this research pass did not capture a primary Microsoft source for the exact kernel registry key the app uses.
setting.professional_notes: The repo research notes list DefaultDynamicHeteroCpuPolicy = 3 and describe the value range 0..7, including 3 = Small and 5 = Dynamic. Microsoft publishes official heterogeneous scheduling policy values for the power-policy SchedulingPolicy and ShortSchedulingPolicy settings, where 0..5 map to all processors, performant processors, prefer performant processors, efficient processors, prefer efficient processors, and automatic. That official material confirms the feature area and that 3 is a meaningful 'efficient processors' style preference, but this research pass did not capture a primary Microsoft source that maps those semantics onto the kernel registry key DefaultDynamicHeteroCpuPolicy. The local Session-Manager trace also shows EnableCpuQuota in the Quota System block, which should not be conflated with DefaultDynamicHeteroCpuPolicy.

allowed_values.label: Primary Microsoft kernel mapping not captured
allowed_values.meaning: This research pass did not capture a primary Microsoft source for the exact DefaultDynamicHeteroCpuPolicy registry semantics.

allowed_values.label: Observed app profile
allowed_values.meaning: The current app treats 3 as the default dynamic hetero CPU policy, with repo notes describing it as a 'Small' preference. Official Microsoft power-policy docs describe 3 as an efficient-processors preference for related scheduling controls, but the exact kernel mapping is still unvalidated.

targets.notes: Tracked as a partially matched heterogeneous-scheduling mapping only.

app_current_implementation.notes: The current app writes DefaultDynamicHeteroCpuPolicy = 3. Microsoft publishes adjacent heterogeneous scheduling policy value meanings, but this research pass has not yet captured a primary Microsoft source for the exact kernel key.

windows_defaults.label: Conservative baseline
windows_defaults.applies_to: Systems where this dataset has not yet validated DefaultDynamicHeteroCpuPolicy from a primary Microsoft kernel source

states.rationale: Do not publish a validated heterogeneous-policy baseline until the exact kernel mapping is backed by a primary Microsoft source.

windows_defaults.notes: This keeps the low-level hybrid-scheduling registry key out of published presets until it is properly sourced.

recommended_profiles.label: Hold for research

states.rationale: Do not publish or apply the value until a primary Microsoft kernel source is captured.

recommended_profiles.good_when: You want to document the hybrid scheduling setting without overstating confidence.
recommended_profiles.bad_when: You need a primary-source Microsoft-backed kernel mapping for DefaultDynamicHeteroCpuPolicy.
tradeoffs: The heterogeneous scheduling feature area is documented honestly.
The kernel registry value stays blocked from validated publication.

The exact kernel mapping remains only partially aligned with official adjacent scheduling policy docs.

evidence.title: Microsoft Learn: SchedulingPolicy
evidence.summary: Microsoft documents official heterogeneous scheduling policy values for the power-policy SchedulingPolicy setting, including 3 = efficient processors. This confirms the feature area and adjacent value semantics, but it does not publish the kernel registry key DefaultDynamicHeteroCpuPolicy.

evidence.title: Repo system research notes for kernel registry values
evidence.summary: The repo documents DefaultDynamicHeteroCpuPolicy = 3 as personal research and lists the local 0..7 value meanings for the kernel variable.

evidence.title: nohuto Session Manager quota-system trace
evidence.summary: Session-Manager.txt lists EnableCpuQuota under the Quota System block, separate from the kernel heterogeneous scheduling policy values.

evidence.title: Current app implementation
evidence.summary: The provider writes DefaultDynamicHeteroCpuPolicy = 3 for the current tweak.

decision.why: Official Microsoft docs confirm adjacent heterogeneous scheduling value meanings, but this research pass did not capture a primary Microsoft source for the exact kernel registry key the app writes. The separate EnableCpuQuota trace in Session Manager is adjacent but not a substitute for the missing kernel mapping.
blocking_issues: No primary Microsoft source for the DefaultDynamicHeteroCpuPolicy kernel registry key was captured in this research pass.
The current app mapping only partially aligns with official adjacent heterogeneous scheduling policy docs.

open_questions: Can a primary Microsoft source for DefaultDynamicHeteroCpuPolicy be captured?
Should the app surface this only as an experimental hybrid-core scheduling setting?

validation_proof.exact_quote_or_path: Microsoft Learn: SchedulingPolicy: Microsoft documents official heterogeneous scheduling policy values for the power-policy SchedulingPolicy setting, including 3 = efficient processors. This confirms the feature area and adjacent value semantics, but it does not publish the kernel registry key DefaultDynamicHeteroCpuPolicy.
validation_proof.notes: Backfilled from evidence_id ms-scheduling-policy (official-doc); deprecated audit trail. See also the local Session-Manager trace for EnableCpuQuota as a separate Quota System value.
